class_schedule.topic: Introduction to Economics and the Market System
learning_objectives: Define economics and the fundamental problem of scarcity
Explain the production possibilities frontier and opportunity cost
Describe how market systems allocate resources through prices

lecture_notes.introduction: Microeconomics studies how individuals, households, and firms make decisions about scarce resources and how those decisions create the outcomes we observe in markets.
key_concepts: Scarcity: unlimited wants vs. limited resources
Opportunity cost: the value of the next best alternative foregone
Production Possibilities Frontier: illustrates tradeoffs and efficiency
Comparative advantage and gains from trade
The invisible hand: how self-interest can lead to social benefit
lecture_notes.demonstration: PPF exercise: plotting production possibilities for two goods and analyzing the effects of technological change and resource changes
setup_requirements: Textbook: Mankiw, Principles of Microeconomics (9th ed.)

review.value: The true cost of any decision is what you give up; measured not in money but in the value of the best alternative foregone
Points on the frontier are efficient; inside are inefficient; outside are unattainable with current resources. The slope represents opportunity cost.

learning_objectives: Construct supply and demand curves from data
Determine market equilibrium price and quantity
Predict the effects of shifts in supply and demand

lecture_notes.introduction: Supply and demand is the most powerful framework in economics. Understanding how markets reach equilibrium and how shifts affect prices and quantities is fundamental.
key_concepts: Demand: law of demand, demand schedule, demand curve
Determinants of demand: income, tastes, prices of related goods, expectations, buyers
Supply: law of supply, supply schedule, supply curve
Market equilibrium: where supply equals demand
Shifts vs. movements: changing determinants vs. changing price
lecture_notes.demonstration: Market equilibrium analysis: determining equilibrium for the coffee market and predicting effects of a drought in Brazil and a health study promoting coffee

review.value: The price at which quantity supplied equals quantity demanded; the market naturally gravitates toward this point through price adjustments
A change in price causes movement along the curve; a change in a determinant other than price shifts the entire curve

learning_objectives: Calculate price elasticity of demand using the midpoint method
Apply elasticity to predict revenue effects of price changes
Distinguish elastic, inelastic, and unit elastic demand

lecture_notes.introduction: Elasticity measures responsiveness. How much does quantity demanded change when price changes? The answer determines whether a firm should raise or lower its price to increase revenue.
key_concepts: Price elasticity of demand = % change in Qd / % change in P
Midpoint method for accurate calculation
Elastic (>1): % change in Q > % change in P
Inelastic (<1): % change in Q < % change in P
Total revenue test: elastic demand means lower price increases revenue
lecture_notes.demonstration: Revenue optimization exercise: calculating elasticity for different products and recommending pricing strategies

review.value: If demand is elastic, lowering price increases total revenue (the volume gain exceeds the price loss). If inelastic, raising price increases revenue.
Availability of substitutes (more substitutes = more elastic), necessity vs. luxury, time horizon (more elastic over time), share of budget

learning_objectives: Explain marginal utility and the law of diminishing marginal utility
Derive demand curves from utility maximization
Apply budget constraints and indifference curves

lecture_notes.introduction: Consumer choice theory explains the foundations of demand. By understanding how consumers maximize satisfaction subject to budget constraints, we can predict how they respond to price and income changes.
key_concepts: Total utility and marginal utility
Law of diminishing marginal utility
Budget constraint: income limits consumption possibilities
Utility maximization: MU_x/P_x = MU_y/P_y
Indifference curves and optimal consumer choice
lecture_notes.demonstration: Utility maximization exercise: allocating a budget across two goods to maximize utility, then showing how a price change alters the optimal bundle

review.value: Each additional unit of a good provides less additional satisfaction; explains why demand curves slope downward
Consumers allocate spending so the marginal utility per dollar is equal across all goods: MU_a/P_a = MU_b/P_b

learning_objectives: Distinguish short-run and long-run production costs
Calculate and graph MC, ATC, AVC curves
Analyze profit maximization in perfectly competitive markets

lecture_notes.introduction: Understanding cost structures is essential for business decisions. The relationship between marginal cost and marginal revenue determines the profit-maximizing output level.
key_concepts: Production function: total product, marginal product, diminishing returns
Short-run costs: fixed, variable, total, average, marginal
MC = change in TC / change in Q; MC intersects ATC at minimum ATC
Profit maximization: produce where MR = MC
Perfect competition: price taker, zero economic profit in long run
lecture_notes.demonstration: Cost curve construction and profit maximization: building cost tables, graphing all curves, and finding optimal output for a competitive firm

review.value: Produce where Marginal Revenue = Marginal Cost; if MR > MC, produce more; if MR < MC, produce less
In perfect competition, entry and exit drive economic profit to zero in the long run; P = MC = minimum ATC

learning_objectives: Analyze monopoly pricing and output decisions
Evaluate monopoly welfare effects: deadweight loss
Apply game theory basics to oligopoly behavior

lecture_notes.introduction: Most real markets fall between perfect competition and monopoly. Understanding market power, pricing strategies, and strategic interaction is essential for business managers.
key_concepts: Monopoly: single seller, unique product, barriers to entry
Monopoly output: MR = MC, but MR < P due to downward-sloping demand
Deadweight loss: monopoly produces less than socially optimal quantity
Price discrimination: first, second, third degree
Oligopoly: few sellers, interdependence, game theory applications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Prisoner's dilemma game and Nash equilibrium: demonstrating why oligopolists face incentives to collude but also to cheat on agreements

review.value: Monopolist produces where MR=MC but charges P > MC; less output and higher price than competitive market, creating deadweight loss
An outcome where no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing strategy; each player's strategy is a best response to the other's

questions.question: The government imposes a $2 per unit tax on sellers of gasoline. Gasoline demand is highly inelastic (elasticity = 0.2). Predict who bears most of the tax burden (consumers or sellers) and explain why.
questions.answer: Consumers bear most of the $2 tax burden. When demand is inelastic, consumers are not very responsive to price changes (few substitutes, gasoline is a necessity). The supply curve shifts up by $2, but because demand is inelastic, the equilibrium price rises by nearly $2 (say $1.80), and quantity falls very little. Sellers pass almost all the tax to consumers through higher prices. The general rule: the more inelastic side of the market bears more of the tax burden because they cannot easily escape the tax by reducing quantity.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies consumers bear most burden
Explains link between elasticity and tax incidence

questions.question: A streaming service raises its monthly price from $10 to $12, and subscriptions drop from 1 million to 900,000. Calculate the price elasticity of demand using the midpoint method and determine if demand is elastic or inelastic.
questions.answer: Midpoint method: % change in Q = (900K - 1M) / ((900K + 1M)/2) = -100K / 950K = -10.53%. % change in P = ($12 - $10) / (($12 + $10)/2) = $2 / $11 = 18.18%. Elasticity = |-10.53% / 18.18%| = 0.58. Since 0.58 < 1, demand is inelastic. This means the price increase will increase total revenue: old revenue = $10M, new revenue = $10.8M.

rubric.key: Correct midpoint calculations for both percentages
Correct elastic/inelastic classification with revenue implication

questions.question: A firm in a perfectly competitive market has the following cost data: at Q=100, ATC=$12, MC=$12, and the market price is $15. Should the firm expand output, and is it earning economic profit? Explain.
questions.answer: Since P ($15) > MC ($12), the firm should expand output. Each additional unit generates more revenue than it costs. The firm should increase production until MC rises to equal P ($15). At Q=100, the firm is earning economic profit: Profit per unit = P - ATC = $15 - $12 = $3. Total profit = $3 x 100 = $300. Note that MC = ATC at Q=100 means the firm is at the minimum of its ATC curve, but since price exceeds this, profit is positive. In the long run, this profit will attract new firms, increasing market supply and driving price down to minimum ATC.

rubric.key: Correct expand decision with MR=MC reasoning

questions.question: Explain why a monopolist faces a downward-sloping marginal revenue curve that lies below its demand curve, while a perfectly competitive firm's MR curve equals its demand curve.
questions.answer: A competitive firm is a price taker: it can sell any quantity at the market price, so MR = P for every unit (MR curve is horizontal at the market price, which is its demand curve). A monopolist faces the entire market demand curve, which slopes downward. To sell one more unit, the monopolist must lower the price on ALL units, not just the marginal unit. So MR = P - (loss on all previous units from lower price). This makes MR < P for every quantity beyond the first unit, and the MR curve lies below the demand curve. Mathematically, if P = a - bQ, then TR = aQ - bQ^2, and MR = a - 2bQ (twice as steep).

rubric.key: Explains competitive firm MR = P correctly
Clear reasoning about price effect on all units

major_assignments.title: Market Analysis and Policy Evaluation
major_assignments.description: Select a real market (industry) and conduct a microeconomic analysis including demand and supply factors, market structure classification, elasticity estimation, and evaluation of a current or proposed government policy affecting that market.
objectives: Apply supply and demand analysis to a real market
Classify the market structure and analyze pricing behavior
Estimate demand elasticity using available data
Evaluate the economic effects of a government policy
deliverables: Supply and demand model with supporting data
Policy evaluation with welfare analysis (consumer/producer surplus)

criteria: Supply and demand factors identified with data
Economic effects analyzed using surplus framework
Deadweight loss or efficiency effects quantified
Rigorous market analysis with real data
Thorough policy evaluation with welfare analysis

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of economic concepts and graphical analysis

problem_sets.description: Weekly quantitative problems covering supply/demand, elasticity, costs, and market structures

major_assignments.description: Market analysis and policy evaluation project

midterm_examination.description: Covers supply and demand, elasticity, and consumer choice

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including production costs and market structures

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance expected. In-class graphing exercises contribute to understanding.
course_policies.late_submissions: Problem sets penalized 10% per day. Market analysis project has firm deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: Problem sets may be discussed but must be submitted individually. Market analysis is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All graphs and calculations must be your own work. Citing economic data sources is required.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Graphing calculator or software. Access to economic data sources (FRED, BLS, Census).
